stewart_read: Leland confirms his wife is on the title. Parker acknowledges ('Okay. Gotcha.') and moves on. The wife is never mentioned again — she is not included in the appointment, not asked about her availability, and not confirmed as a participant in the closer call. The appointment is booked for Leland alone. Strategic hypothesis: Parker intended to address spouse inclusion at button-up and forgot. Reactive hypothesis: Parker treated this as a data point rather than a protocol trigger. The reactive read holds — there is no later move that addresses the wife's inclusion.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have planned to address spouse inclusion at button-up but the bill navigation consumed the call's attention.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. The schema is explicit: wife on title means wife must be on the appointment. Parker had the information at 02:11 and never acted on it. The closer will walk into a sit where the co-decision-maker may not be present. Ask Parker: 'What's the protocol when the customer says their spouse is on the title?' Then walk through the correct response per protocols.spouse_decision.

quote: Well, yeah, definitely see if we can do that. You know, at least cut that back for you, make it a little bit more stable and steady for you, save you some money there.
stewart_read: Parker captures the bill range ($100 low, $200s high) and responds with a generic savings promise. The schema's capture-and-flip pattern requires the bill to be turned into emotional alignment against the utility — Parker does the capture and drops the flip entirely. This is the universal floor failure mode the schema names explicitly.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have been holding the flip for the closer's conversation, keeping setter scope narrow. But the schema's bill_anchoring section is explicit that the flip is the setter's job, not the closer's.
coaching_implication: This is the floor-wide coaching priority the schema flags. Show Parker the difference between 'capture and drop' and 'capture and flip.' A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): acknowledge the range, then redirect the pain toward the utility — something like 'So Xcel is taking anywhere from $100 to $200-plus from you every month, and that's before whatever back-charges they're adding. That's exactly the kind of thing we're built to fix.' No fabricated numbers — use only what Leland stated.

quote: I just got a bill for $584. And I wonder what — so I think that's probably — they're back charging me. Yeah. Because amount due, $1,039.26.
stewart_read: Leland discovers a $1,039 back-charge in real time on the call. This is a financial vulnerability disclosure — the schema's explicit category. Parker's response is a navigation prompt ('do you see, like, a usage history or a KWH usage or anything like that?'). The $1,039 figure receives zero acknowledgment. This is the call's highest-leverage empathy moment and the rep walks straight past it. Note: Leland is resilient — he self-recovers at 10:53 ('I would've been shocked, you know, if I didn't know what this was about'), which means the appointment survived. But the trust cost is real and the closer inherits it.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have judged that engaging the billing dispute would derail the call into scope creep. But the schema distinguishes empathy acknowledgment from scope engagement — a one-sentence acknowledgment ('That's a rough surprise to find mid-call') costs nothing and builds trust.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the audio from 08:34 to 09:05. Ask Parker: 'What did Leland just tell you?' Then ask: 'What did you say back?' The gap is visible. The coaching move is not 'be more empathetic' — it's specific: when a customer discovers a financial surprise on the call, acknowledge the number before asking for navigation help. A suggested bridge (intent, not verbatim): 'That's a significant bill to land on — I get why you'd want to sort that out. While we're here, let's see if we can find the usage history so the design reflects your real consumption, not the billing error.' This keeps scope, adds empathy, and bridges to solar's value as a hedge against utility unpredictability.

quote: Oh, yeah. I would've been shocked, you know, if I didn't know what this was about.
stewart_read: Leland self-recovers from the billing anomaly and contextualizes the September 5,104 kWh spike with calm self-awareness. This is an enthusiasm signal: a customer who can articulate his own usage patterns, who has been tracking his bill closely enough to notice the discrepancy, and who is not rattled by the complexity. He is a high-engagement, high-retention prospect. Parker responds with 'Oh, wow. Okay. Gotcha. Yeah. That's a big jump there.' — functional but flat. The moment was an opening to anchor the utility's unreliability as the exact problem solar solves.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have read this as a closing signal and moved to wrap the bill collection rather than extend the conversation. That read is defensible — the call was already booked. But the flip opportunity was present and unused.
coaching_implication: Show Parker that Leland's self-recovery line is a gift, not just a data point. A customer who says 'I would've been shocked if I didn't know what this was about' is telling you he's been paying close attention to his utility relationship — and that relationship has already surprised him once. That's the exact emotional hook for solar's stability pitch. The manager doesn't need to make this a long coaching beat — just flag it as a missed anchor and ask Parker what he could have said.

quote: they would give you a call kinda like we're talking now, and then they just ask that you can have an email or a computer or something pulled up so then they'll email you the design while they're on the phone with you. Would that work for you?
stewart_read: Parker executes a partial button-up mid-call — he anchors the computer requirement and gets a verbal confirmation ('Sure'). This is credit-worthy: the computer anchor is present and confirmed. However, it is delivered mid-call before the bill is received, not restated at close, and the full button-up checklist is incomplete at call end: no 'one hour block' statement, no 'not driving' anchor, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake. The partial execution mid-call is not a substitute for the complete protocol at close.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have judged that restating the computer requirement at close would feel redundant given Leland's 'Sure' confirmation. But the schema's button_up section treats the close-of-call restatement as a no-show-rate predictor, not a courtesy.
coaching_implication: Credit Parker for the mid-call computer anchor — that's a real execution. Then show him the gap: the button-up protocol exists at close for a reason. Walk through the required elements (one-hour block, not driving, 'is that fair' handshake) and ask Parker to practice the full sequence. The coaching point is sequencing and completeness, not the individual elements.

quote: I've been interested in, solar for a long time, and a lot of my neighbors have it. And, you know, with this oil business, I thought, well, maybe this is a good time to look into it seriously.
stewart_read: Leland volunteers a three-factor motivation (long-standing interest, neighbor social proof, macro energy context) in response to a single open question. This is a depth signal — the schema's verify.interest_in_solar section explicitly grades depth and notes that top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Parker responds with a brief acknowledgment and pivots immediately to roof assessment. The emotional anchor is present and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have read the response as sufficient qualification and moved to keep the call efficient. The call does book in under 12 minutes, which is a positive outcome. But the schema is explicit that depth here matters more than the answer — Parker had a customer ready to talk and moved on.
coaching_implication: This is a teaching moment on the difference between checking the box and building the anchor. Ask Parker: 'When Leland said he'd been interested for a long time and his neighbors have it — what did you do with that?' The answer is 'I moved on.' The coaching move: one follow-up question ('What's been holding you back from pulling the trigger until now?') would have surfaced the real emotional driver and given Parker a tieback for the text follow-up and the closer's brief.

quote: Perfect. Well, thanks for taking the time with me today, Leland. We'll talk to you tomorrow, and then I'll send you a text now just with the date and time, so, like, tomorrow at 9AM just so you have it. And if anything comes up or any questions, just feel free to shoot me a text message, and I'll be sure to respond to you as fast as I can.
stewart_read: The close-out is warm and functional but the button-up protocol is incomplete at the point it matters most. Missing at close: the one-hour block statement, the 'not driving' anchor, and the 'is that fair' verbal handshake. The computer anchor was given mid-call (07:32) but not restated. Additionally, the bill photo has not been received by call end — the appointment is booked on the expectation that Leland will send it, not on confirmed receipt. The text follow-up promise is made but no tieback to Leland's stated interests is included per text_after_call requirements.
alternative_hypothesis: Parker may have judged the close-out sufficient given Leland's cooperative tone throughout. Leland's 'Sounds good' (11:33) suggests he is not resistant. But the schema's button_up section treats these elements as no-show-rate predictors, not rapport signals.
coaching_implication: Walk Parker through the button-up checklist against this close-out. The gap is not attitude — Parker's warmth is genuine. The gap is protocol completeness. Ask Parker to write out the full button-up sequence from memory, then compare to the schema. The 'is that fair' handshake in particular is the one element most predictive of show rate and the one most commonly skipped.
